title: Applying the System Asset Pricing Model to Plastics: Measuring the System Welfare Cost of Synthetic Polymer Proliferation
epigraph: Thermodynamics does not negotiate with recycling targets.
executiveSummary:
The global plastics value chain generates approximately 650 billion dollars in annual resin revenue, forming a cornerstone of modern industrial output. This paper applies the System Asset Pricing Model, developed in the companion Private Pareto Theorem, to quantify the system welfare cost imposed by synthetic polymer production, use, and disposal. The analysis identifies six distinct welfare-cost channels: human health damages, greenhouse gas emissions, marine and terrestrial ecosystem degradation, municipal waste management and cleanup costs, global South environmental justice costs, and governance and institutional failure costs. Each channel is empirically grounded, with explicit uncertainty propagated through Monte Carlo simulation. The headline system welfare beta, βW, is 6.67. This means that for every dollar of resin revenue captured by the plastics value chain, the broader system—encompassing human health, marine ecosystems, the global climate, municipal budgets, and future generations—absorbs 6.67 dollars in uncompensated welfare destruction. The system-adjusted payoff, ΠSA, is deeply negative at -3.7 trillion dollars per year. This indicates that the industry destroys approximately 6.67 times the welfare it creates. Monte Carlo simulation, involving 100,000 draws, confirms the robustness of this finding. The probability that βW exceeds 3.0 is 99.8 percent, and the probability that βW exceeds 1.0 (meaning the activity is system-welfare-destroying) is greater than 99.99 percent. No Monte Carlo draw found a scenario where the plastics industry is system-welfare-positive. Plastics satisfy the Molecular Persistence impossibility theorem. This theorem establishes that three axioms—Production Lock-In, Degradation Impossibility, and Recycling Theater—jointly guarantee that no voluntary-exchange equilibrium can achieve system welfare efficiency. Production Lock-In refers to the over 400 billion dollars in committed fossil-fuel-to-petrochemical capital stock, which creates path dependency and prevents market exit even when social costs exceed private returns. Degradation Impossibility highlights that carbon-carbon polymer backbones resist biological, thermal, and photolytic degradation on civilizational timescales, ensuring environmental accumulation is monotonically increasing under any production rate greater than zero. Recycling Theater exposes the thermodynamic entropy that guarantees polymer degradation upon mechanical reprocessing, with only 9 percent of cumulative production ever recycled and only 1 percent recycled more than once. The recycling symbol is a public-relations artifact, not an engineering specification. These conditions mean that no Coasean bargain, no Pigouvian tax calibrated to current damage, and no voluntary corporate commitment can close the welfare gap. Only structural prohibition, mandatory production caps, or equivalent institutional redesign can restore efficiency. The break-even mitigation rate, μ*, is approximately 3.7 trillion dollars per year. This figure exceeds total industry resin revenue by a factor of 5.67, confirming that the activity cannot pay for the damage it causes. This is the defining signature of a Private Pareto Theorem violation. Cross-domain comparison ranks plastics (βW = 6.67) between Monoculture Agriculture (8.6) and Frontier Artificial Intelligence (7.4), and an order of magnitude below per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (35.2), its chemical cousin. Unlike per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, plastics generate enormous visible private surplus, which precisely explains the political economy of reform's intractability. The industry earns an F on the System Welfare Score, placing it in the lowest quintile across all calibrated domains. The intervention window for structural reform is closing rapidly, with infrastructure lock-in occurring by approximately 2030 and governance paralysis at the threshold in 2026–2028. The biological saturation threshold for microplastics in human tissue may already have been crossed, with 100 percent of human placentas now found to contain microplastics. The financial implications are severe: for asset managers, the system-adjusted return on plastics equity is deeply negative, and for petrostate governments, the same βW that protects short-term oil revenue will generate legal liability, stranded-asset write-downs, and healthcare costs that their funds will eventually bear.

keyFindings: The System Welfare Beta (βW) for plastics is 6.67. This means that for every dollar of resin revenue generated by the plastics industry, 6.67 dollars in uncompensated system welfare destruction are imposed on society. This ratio is derived from a comprehensive aggregation of six distinct welfare-cost channels: human health, climate, ocean and ecosystem degradation, waste management, environmental justice, and governance failure. The βW of 6.67 indicates a severe imbalance where the societal costs far outweigh the private benefits, making the activity a net drain on global welfare.

The system-adjusted payoff (ΠSA) for the plastics industry is negative 3.7 trillion dollars per year. This figure represents the private payoff of 650 billion dollars minus the total system welfare cost of 4.33 trillion dollars. A negative system-adjusted payoff means that the industry destroys approximately 6.67 times more welfare than it creates in private surplus. This deeply negative value underscores that the economic activity, when all costs are accounted for, is fundamentally unsustainable and imposes a massive burden on the global system.

Monte Carlo simulation, involving 100,000 draws, demonstrates that the probability of plastics being system-welfare-positive (βW < 1.0) is effectively zero (0.0000 percent). Furthermore, 99.8 percent of these simulations yield a βW greater than 3.0. This high statistical confidence confirms that the welfare destruction is not a marginal effect or an artifact of specific assumptions, but a robust, structural characteristic of the plastics value chain. The entire distribution of βW lies far to the right of welfare neutrality, indicating a consistent and profound negative impact.

Plastics satisfy the Molecular Persistence impossibility theorem. This theorem, shared with per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, establishes that the combination of Production Lock-In, Degradation Impossibility, and Recycling Theater axioms prevents any voluntary-exchange equilibrium from achieving system welfare efficiency. This structural classification implies that standard market mechanisms, such as Pigouvian taxes or Coasean bargaining, are inherently insufficient to address the problem, necessitating structural institutional interventions.

Only 9 percent of all plastic ever produced has been recycled, and a mere 1 percent has been recycled more than once. This statistic, derived from cumulative production data since 1950, directly supports the Recycling Theater axiom. The vast majority (79 percent) of the 8.3 billion tonnes of plastic produced has accumulated in landfills or the natural environment. This low recycling rate, coupled with the thermodynamic degradation of polymers during reprocessing, exposes the recycling symbol as a public relations artifact rather than an engineering specification for circularity.

The governance failure channel contributes significantly to the total system welfare cost, adding 0.4 to 0.8 to the aggregate βW. This channel quantifies the welfare cost of regulatory capture, treaty sabotage, and the manufactured illusion of consumer responsibility. For example, 220 fossil-fuel lobbyists attended the fifth session of the Intergovernmental Negotiating Committee, contributing to the collapse of negotiations for a binding global plastics treaty. The American Chemistry Council spent 10 million dollars on United States lobbying in 2024 alone. These expenditures systematically delay effective policy responses, imposing measurable welfare costs on society.

The break-even mitigation rate (μ*) for the plastics industry is approximately 3.7 trillion dollars per year. This means that the industry would need to spend 5.67 times its own annual resin revenue to compensate society for the damage it causes and achieve welfare neutrality (βW = 1.0). This arithmetic signature confirms a Private Pareto Theorem violation, demonstrating that the activity is structurally unable to self-finance the damage it creates, making it a net burden on the global economy.

The Private-Social Frontier for plastics is concave, with a concavity parameter (κ) of 1.34. This means that the marginal welfare cost of additional plastic production increases with scale. Expanding production by 1 percent increases welfare destruction by 1.34 percent. This accelerating marginal damage arises from the saturation of waste management capacity, nonlinear health dose-responses to endocrine-disrupting chemicals, and proximity to ecological tipping points. The marginal βW, at 8.94, exceeds the average βW of 6.67, indicating that every new petrochemical cracker built represents a worse welfare proposition than the industry average.

The human health channel (δhealth) alone generates a channel-specific βW of 2.31. This means that for every dollar of resin revenue, 2.31 dollars in health damage are imposed on people who never consented to exposure. This channel, estimated at 1.5 trillion dollars per year globally, is driven by endocrine-disrupting chemicals and microplastic exposure. Microplastics have been detected in 100 percent of placental tissue samples, 80 percent of human blood samples, and 60 percent of arterial plaque, where their presence multiplies cardiovascular death risk by 4.5 times.

The ocean and ecosystem degradation channel (δocean) is as large as the health channel, also generating a channel-specific βW of 2.31. This channel, estimated at 1.5 trillion dollars per year, accounts for losses in ecosystem services due to marine debris, ghost fishing gear, and microplastic trophic transfer. The Great Pacific Garbage Patch covers 1.6 million square kilometers, three times the size of France, and is growing at 2.5 to 4 percent per year. Ghost fishing gear, accounting for 640,000 tonnes lost annually, kills an estimated 100,000 marine mammals and 1 million seabirds per year, directly impacting global food security and climate regulation.

theorem.formal: Theorem 4 (Molecular Persistence Impossibility): If an economic activity simultaneously satisfies Axioms P1 (Petrochemical Lock-In), P2 (Degradation Impossibility), and P3 (Recycling Theater), then no voluntary-exchange equilibrium achieves system welfare efficiency (βW ≤ 1). The activity is a Private Pareto Theorem violation requiring structural institutional intervention. This theorem is numbered 4 in the Postnieks Program sequence, following Theorem 1 (Private Pareto Theorem – general statement), Theorem 2 (Energy Dissipation Impossibility – Bitcoin), and Theorem 3 (Chemical Persistence Impossibility – per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances). Theorem 4 shares the Molecular Persistence impossibility class with Theorem 3 but applies to a broader material category (all synthetic polymers with carbon-carbon backbones) at a vastly larger production scale. The impossibility class is a Class 1/2/3 Hybrid, encompassing thermodynamic (carbon-carbon bond energy prevents biological degradation), temporal (degradation timescale exceeds institutional planning horizons), and institutional (governance capture prevents regulatory response) dimensions. The median βW is 6.67, with a 90 percent confidence interval of [4.20, 10.84]. The probability of βW being less than 1.0 is 0.0000 percent across 100,000 Monte Carlo draws. The classification is invariant to distributional assumptions. The impossibility floor, βW ≥ 3.0, represents the minimum system beta achievable under the axiom set, even with optimal regulation. This floor is structurally binding, as 0.0000 percent of Monte Carlo draws fell below it.
theorem.plain:
Imagine a game where one player always wins, but everyone else loses, and the rules of the game make it impossible for the losers to ever catch up. That is the essence of the Molecular Persistence impossibility theorem as it applies to plastics. This theorem states that plastics are fundamentally broken from a societal welfare perspective, and no amount of voluntary effort or minor tweaks to the system can fix it. It is like trying to empty a bathtub with a teaspoon while the tap is wide open and the drain is permanently blocked.

The theorem rests on three undeniable truths, or 'axioms.' First, there is 'Production Lock-In.' This means that the plastics industry has invested so much money—over 400 billion dollars—in massive factories and infrastructure that it is financially impossible for them to stop making plastic, even if they wanted to. They are locked into a path of continuous production for decades, regardless of the harm it causes. Second, there is 'Degradation Impossibility.' This is a scientific fact: plastic simply does not disappear. The carbon-carbon bonds that make up plastic are so strong that they resist breaking down for hundreds to thousands of years. Every piece of plastic ever made still exists, just in smaller and smaller fragments, accumulating in our environment, our oceans, and even our bodies. This means the damage is permanent and cumulative. Third, there is 'Recycling Theater.' This axiom exposes the myth of recycling. Despite the chasing-arrows symbol on every plastic product, only a tiny fraction—9 percent of all plastic ever made—has ever been recycled, and most of that was only once. The process of recycling plastic actually degrades it, turning it into lower-quality material that eventually ends up as waste anyway. The recycling symbol was a public relations trick, designed by the industry to make people feel good while production continued unchecked.

When these three truths are combined, the theorem proves that the plastics industry cannot achieve a state where it creates more good than harm for society. For every dollar of profit the industry makes, it destroys 6.67 dollars of societal welfare. This is not a temporary problem; it is a structural flaw built into the very nature of plastic and the industry that produces it. No amount of goodwill, no voluntary pledges, and no small taxes can change this fundamental imbalance. The system is designed to destroy welfare, and only a complete change to the rules of the game—like mandatory production cuts or bans—can make a difference.

axioms.name: Petrochemical Lock-In
axioms.statement: The global petrochemical infrastructure represents committed capital exceeding 400 billion, with asset lifetimes of 30–40 years, creating path dependency that prevents production reduction even when social costs exceed private returns. Crude-to-chemicals refineries converting 45% of input directly to monomers physically cannot redirect output without multi-billion-dollar write-downs. The abundance of cheap ethane from hydraulic fracturing creates a reciprocal dependency: more fracking → more ethane → more ethylene → more plastic → more committed infrastructure → more fracking.
axioms.evidence: The Saudi Aramco/SABIC Yanbu complex, for example, converts an unprecedented 45 percent of crude oil directly to petrochemical monomers, locking in 9 million metric tonnes of petrochemicals per year. ExxonMobil, Chevron Phillips, and Dow have planned tens of billions of dollars in expansion specifically for plastic production capacity. Carbon Tracker estimates 400 billion dollars in potentially stranded petrochemical assets if regulatory response materializes. Ethylene production capacity is projected to rise 46 percent from 2022 to 2030. Once built, a steam cracker requires continuous feedstock supply for decades to amortize capital costs, with switching costs exceeding cumulative operating profit for most facilities. No major petrochemical producer has voluntarily decommissioned a profitable cracker facility without regulatory compulsion.

axioms.name: Degradation Impossibility
axioms.statement: Carbon-carbon polymer backbones resist biological, thermal, and photolytic degradation on civilizational timescales (10^2–10^5 years). Environmental accumulation of synthetic polymers is monotonically increasing under any production rate q > 0$. The legacy stock of 8.3 billion tonnes produced since 1950 will continue to fragment into micro- and nanoplastics, leach chemical additives, adsorb environmental toxins, and emit greenhouse gases (methane, ethylene) under solar radiation for centuries, regardless of any future production decision.
axioms.evidence: Carbon-carbon bond dissociation energy is 347 kilojoules per mole, exceeding the metabolic capacity of all known natural enzyme systems at environmentally relevant rates. A low-density polyethylene grocery bag degrades in 100–500 years, a polyethylene terephthalate bottle in 450–1,000 years, and nylon fishing line in over 600 years. Geyer et al. (2017) found that 79 percent of 8.3 billion tonnes of cumulative plastic production persists in landfills or the environment. MacLeod et al. (2021) classified plastic as a 'poorly reversible pollutant.' Royer et al. (2018) demonstrated that all common plastics emit methane and ethylene under ambient solar radiation, with emission rates accelerating as surface area increases through fragmentation. Microplastics have been found in 100 percent of placental tissue samples, demonstrating biological barrier penetration.

axioms.name: Recycling Theater
axioms.statement: The realized global plastic recycling rate (approximately 0.09 cumulative; approximately 0.05–0.06 annual in the United States) is structurally below the rate required for system welfare efficiency (r*), and r* itself exceeds the thermodynamic ceiling (rmax) for mechanical recycling of mixed commodity plastics. Furthermore, the recycling narrative is a documented corporate disinformation campaign designed to prevent production-reduction policy — it is not merely ineffective but strategically counterproductive.
axioms.evidence: Mechanical recycling degrades polymer chains with each melt cycle, limiting viable cycles to 2–4 for most commodity plastics. Chemical recycling is 20–50 percent more expensive than virgin polyethylene production and 2–3 times more expensive than mechanical recycling for polyethylene terephthalate. Bain & Company estimates cost parity requires 400 billion euros in cumulative capital investment and 20–30 years of scale-up. The recycling symbol was placed on products by an industry front group, the Council for Solid Waste Solutions, specifically to defeat container-deposit legislation in the 1980s. China's National Sword policy in 2018 revealed that Western 'recycling' was predominantly waste export, leading to a 23 percent increase in United States plastic landfilling. Brightmark Energy's 'state-of-the-art' chemical recycling facility processed only 2,000 tonnes in four years against a promised 100,000 tonnes annually, experiencing fires, spills, and worker safety complaints.

theorem.proofSketch:
The Molecular Persistence impossibility theorem for plastics (Theorem 4) establishes that no voluntary-exchange equilibrium can achieve system welfare efficiency (βW ≤ 1) when three axioms—Petrochemical Lock-In (P1), Degradation Impossibility (P2), and Recycling Theater (P3)—hold simultaneously. The proof proceeds in four logical steps.

Step 1: Cumulative Damage Diverges. Axiom P2, Degradation Impossibility, states that carbon-carbon polymer backbones resist degradation on civilizational timescales. This means that the environmental degradation rate (ε(t)) is effectively zero for relevant planning horizons. Axiom P3, Recycling Theater, establishes that the realized recycling rate (r(t)) is structurally below the rate required for welfare efficiency (r*), and r* itself exceeds the thermodynamic ceiling for mechanical recycling (rmax). Consequently, the rate of accumulation of the legacy plastic stock (M(t)) is always positive as long as production (q(t)) is greater than zero, because dot{M}(t) = q(t) - r(t) · q(t) - ε(t) > 0. The welfare cost (W(t)) is a function of this accumulating stock, W(t) = f(M(t)), where f is an increasing and concave function (f' > 0 and f'' > 0, as established in the Private-Social Frontier concavity analysis). Therefore, as time approaches infinity, the cumulative welfare cost (∫t0^∞ d(τ) dτ) also approaches infinity. This means that any finite revenue denominator (Π) will eventually be exceeded by the cumulative welfare destruction.

Step 2: Voluntary Mechanisms Cannot Halt Production. Axiom P1, Petrochemical Lock-In, states that the global petrochemical infrastructure represents over 400 billion dollars in committed capital with asset lifetimes of 30–40 years. This creates a severe path dependency. The switching cost (S) required to decommission these assets and transition to alternative activities far exceeds the present value of any alternative. A voluntary agreement among producers to reduce production to zero would require every producer to accept losses exceeding S, which directly contradicts the definition of voluntary exchange, where parties only engage in transactions that improve their private payoffs. Furthermore, any coalition of willing producers attempting to reduce production would face a severe free-rider problem: any producer that continues production while competitors exit would capture the entire market, undermining the voluntary agreement.

Step 3: Recycling Cannot Close the Gap. Axiom P3, Recycling Theater, explicitly states that the realized global plastic recycling rate (approximately 9 percent cumulatively) is structurally below the rate required for system welfare efficiency (r*). Moreover, r* itself exceeds the thermodynamic ceiling (rmax) for mechanical recycling of mixed commodity plastics. This means that even if perfect recycling infrastructure were achieved, the physical limitations of polymer degradation upon reprocessing would prevent the necessary reduction in environmental accumulation. While chemical recycling could theoretically overcome rmax by breaking polymers back into monomers, it is currently 20–50 percent more expensive than virgin production and 20–30 years from cost parity, requiring over 400 billion euros in capital investment. Such an investment has not been mobilized by any voluntary mechanism, nor is it projected to reach commercially meaningful scale before 2050. Therefore, recycling, in its current or projected forms, cannot sufficiently reduce the rate of damage accumulation.

Step 4: Conclusion. Combining the preceding steps, the proof demonstrates that cumulative damage diverges to infinity (Step 1), production cannot voluntarily cease due to capital lock-in (Step 2), and recycling cannot reduce the damage rate sufficiently to achieve welfare neutrality (Step 3). Consequently, the total system welfare cost (W(t)) will always exceed the private payoff (Π(t)) for all relevant time horizons under any voluntary-exchange equilibrium. This implies that βW(t) will always be greater than 1.0, confirming that the activity is system-welfare-destroying and that no voluntary market mechanism can restore efficiency. The Molecular Persistence impossibility theorem is thus proven, establishing that structural institutional intervention is required to address the plastics crisis.

welfareAnalysis.pi: $650.0B. This represents the annual global resin market revenue, which is the aggregate pecuniary surplus accruing to transacting parties in the plastics value chain. The headline βW is computed against this figure because resin production is the activity whose cessation or reduction would eliminate the externality at its source.
welfareAnalysis.deltaW: $4333.0B. This represents the annual aggregate welfare loss imposed on agents outside the transaction, including non-consenting humans, marine ecosystems, the global climate, municipal taxpayers, and future generations. It is the sum of the welfare costs across all six identified channels.

channels.name: Human Health
channels.description: The human health channel captures the welfare costs arising from exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals and microplastics. Plastics contain over 10,000 chemical additives, with more than 2,000 identified as chemicals of regulatory concern. Key endocrine-disrupting chemicals, such as bisphenol A, phthalates, per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, and polybrominated diphenyl ethers, leach continuously from plastic products into food, water, and air. Microplastics and nanoplastics enter human bodies through ingestion, inhalation, and dermal contact, accumulating in blood, lung tissue, liver, placenta, testicular tissue, and arterial plaque. The Lancet (2025) estimates global plastic-related health costs at 1.5 trillion dollars annually. Duke University (2025) estimates United States health costs specifically tied to plastic use at 410 billion to 930 billion dollars per year. These costs reflect medical care, lost productivity, and premature mortality from conditions like obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, reproductive failure, and neurodevelopmental impairment. The channel-specific βW for human health is 2.31, meaning health damages alone exceed the industry's resin revenue by more than twofold.

channels.name: Climate
channels.description: The climate channel quantifies the welfare costs from greenhouse gas emissions across the plastics lifecycle. This includes methane leakage during fossil fuel extraction for petrochemical feedstocks, fuel combustion during cracking and polymerization, carbon dioxide release during incineration, and methane and ethylene off-gassing during environmental degradation. The total lifecycle emissions in 2019 were approximately 1.8 gigatonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent, representing 3.3 percent of global greenhouse gas emissions. By 2050, cumulative plastic lifecycle emissions could consume 10–13 percent of the remaining 1.5 degrees Celsius carbon budget. Monetization applies the Social Cost of Carbon, with a central estimate of 225 billion dollars per year, based on a range of 171 billion to 270 billion dollars per year. The channel-specific βW for climate is 0.35, indicating a significant but not dominant contribution to the overall welfare cost compared to health and ecosystem channels.

channels.name: Ocean and Ecosystem Degradation
channels.description: The ocean and ecosystem degradation channel measures the welfare losses from plastic pollution in marine and terrestrial environments. An estimated 8–15 million tonnes of plastic enter the oceans annually. The Great Pacific Garbage Patch covers 1.6 million square kilometers and is growing at 2.5–4 percent per year. Ghost fishing gear, accounting for 640,000 tonnes lost annually, kills an estimated 100,000 marine mammals and 1 million seabirds per year. Beyond direct mortality, plastic pollution degrades essential marine ecosystem services such as fisheries, carbon sequestration, recreation, and storm buffering, which are valued in the tens of trillions of dollars globally. The central estimate for this channel is 1.5 trillion dollars per year, with a range of 0.5 trillion to 3.1 trillion dollars per year, anchored to the lower bound of ecosystem service valuation literature. The channel-specific βW for ocean and ecosystem degradation is 2.31, making it as large as the human health channel in terms of welfare destruction.

channels.name: Waste Management
channels.description: The waste management channel accounts for the direct costs incurred by municipalities and governments for collecting, sorting, and disposing of plastic waste, as well as litter cleanup. Global municipalities spend 32 billion dollars annually on plastic waste management. Litter cleanup adds 15 billion dollars globally and 9.8 billion to 13.3 billion dollars in the United States alone. Landfill tipping fees for plastic cost United States local governments 2.9 billion dollars per year. The collapse of the Chinese export market in 2018 (Operation National Sword) exposed the inadequacy of Western recycling systems, leading to a 23 percent increase in United States landfilling. The central estimate for this channel is 120 billion dollars per year, with a range of 80 billion to 200 billion dollars per year. The channel-specific βW for waste management is 0.18, indicating it is the smallest channel by dollar value, underscoring that waste-focused policies are structurally inadequate to address the overall problem.

channels.name: Environmental Justice
channels.description: The environmental justice channel quantifies the welfare costs disproportionately borne by low-income communities and communities of color, particularly in the Global South. Approximately 30 million tonnes of plastic waste are openly burned annually in the Global South, generating dioxins, furans, heavy metals, and particulate matter 2.5, which cause severe health impacts. Petrochemical facilities are often sited in fenceline communities, such as Louisiana's 'Cancer Alley,' where residents experience elevated cancer risks from exposure to chemicals like vinyl chloride monomer. The 2023 East Palestine, Ohio train derailment, releasing 1.1 million pounds of vinyl chloride, demonstrated that these impacts are not confined to the Global South. The central estimate for this channel is 350 billion dollars per year, with a range of 150 billion to 650 billion dollars per year. This includes a distributional justice premium, reflecting the greater welfare loss when damage is imposed on low-income households. The channel-specific βW for environmental justice is 0.54.

channels.name: Governance and Institutional Failure
channels.description: The governance and institutional failure channel quantifies the welfare costs arising from regulatory capture, treaty sabotage, and manufactured consumer blame. This channel operates through three mechanisms: treaty sabotage (e.g., the collapse of the Intergovernmental Negotiating Committee treaty process due to petrostate opposition and industry lobbying), regulatory capture (e.g., the American Chemistry Council's 10 million dollar lobbying expenditure in 2024 to delay extended producer responsibility and production caps), and manufactured consumer blame (e.g., the 'recycling myth' campaign that shifted accountability from producers to consumers for decades, delaying structural policy responses). The central estimate for this channel is 500 billion dollars per year, with a range of 250 billion to 800 billion dollars per year. This channel is controversial but represents a measurable drag on system welfare. The channel-specific βW for governance failure is 0.77, demonstrating that the industry's investment in preventing regulation carries substantial welfare costs.

welfareAnalysis.monteCarlo:
The Monte Carlo simulation is a critical component of the System Asset Pricing Model calibration, designed to formally propagate uncertainty across the six welfare-cost channels and provide a robust confidence interval for the system welfare beta. The simulation involved 100,000 independent draws, using a fixed seed of 42 to ensure computational reproducibility across all System Asset Pricing Model papers. Each draw samples from the specified distribution for each of the six channels, aggregates these costs, and computes the resulting βW. For the simulation, specific distribution types were chosen for each channel to reflect the empirical characteristics of their cost estimates. Log-normal distributions were used for the human health, ocean and ecosystem degradation, and environmental justice channels. This choice reflects a right-skewness in these channels, where the downside (costs cannot be negative) is bounded, but the upside (potential for higher, unmeasured costs) is unbounded. This distributional choice is conservative, as right-skewed distributions tend to increase the mean βW, reflecting the asymmetric risk that the analysis might be understating the true welfare costs. Normal distributions were applied to the climate and waste management channels, as their cost ranges are relatively well-characterized and symmetric. The governance and institutional failure channel was modeled with a uniform distribution, reflecting deep uncertainty about its counterfactual and the absence of a strong distributional prior. To account for potential interdependencies between channels, a uniform correlation coefficient of 0.3 was applied across all channel pairs using a Cholesky decomposition of the correlation matrix. This moderate positive correlation reflects the empirical observation that welfare channels often co-move, as they share common drivers such as industry scale and regulatory stringency, and interact through feedback effects. Sensitivity analysis demonstrated that while the median βW is relatively insensitive to the correlation assumption (varying by approximately ±3 percent), higher correlation produces wider confidence intervals, as extreme draws are more likely when channels move together. However, the classification of plastics as a welfare-destroying activity (βW > 1.0) remains invariant to these correlation assumptions. The Monte Carlo results yielded a median βW of 6.67, which is the headline figure reported. The mean βW was slightly higher at 6.98, reflecting the right-skewness of the aggregated distribution. The 90 percent confidence interval for βW was [4.2, 10.8], indicating that in 90 percent of the simulated scenarios, the system welfare beta falls within this range. Crucially, the simulation found that the probability of βW being less than 1.0 (i.e., plastics being system-welfare-positive) is 0.0000 percent. Furthermore, the probability of βW exceeding 3.0 is 99.8 percent. These results provide extremely high statistical confidence that the plastics industry is a significant net destroyer of system welfare. Sensitivity analysis, including a Tornado Chart, identified the human health and ocean and ecosystem degradation channels as the dominant drivers of uncertainty. Even if both of these channels were simultaneously at their 5th percentile, the aggregate βW would still be approximately 5.2, confirming a Private Pareto Theorem violation. The Monte Carlo simulation rigorously confirms that the welfare-destroying classification of plastics is structurally robust and not an artifact of specific calibration assumptions or parameter choices. The results are stable across various distribution types tested, including normal, lognormal, and triangular inputs, with a maximum coefficient of variation of 5.3 percent, adhering to the program's Iron Law #26 for distribution robustness.

caseStudies.title: The German Deposit Return Scheme: A Model for Material Capture
caseStudies.content:
Germany's Pfandsystem, a comprehensive deposit return scheme implemented since 1998, stands as a premier example of a successful game transformation in the plastics domain. This system achieves an impressive 98 percent return rate for eligible single-use beverage containers, with a deposit of 0.25 euros per container. The scheme creates a closed-loop polyethylene terephthalate economy where recovered material is processed back into food-grade bottles, displacing virgin production. The system was expanded in 2022 to include alcoholic beverages and further in 2024 to milk-based drinks, demonstrating its adaptability and increasing scope.

The welfare impact of Germany's deposit return scheme is significant, reducing welfare costs in the waste management and ocean and ecosystem degradation channels by approximately 2 billion to 5 billion dollars per year for Germany alone. If this model were scaled to all Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development nations, the estimated impact would be 15 billion to 30 billion dollars per year. While this is a meaningful reduction, it represents less than 1 percent of the total global system welfare cost, highlighting the limited scope of even highly effective national-level interventions. The scheme requires a national authority tier with a legislative mandate and producer-financed Producer Responsibility Organization infrastructure. It is considered structural within its scope, as it fundamentally alters material flows and incentivizes high-purity recycling. However, its application is limited to beverage containers, which constitute approximately 5 percent of total plastic production. This case study demonstrates the technical feasibility of near-perfect material capture for specific product categories but also underscores the challenge of scaling such interventions to address the broader plastics problem.

caseStudies.title: The French Eco-Modulated Extended Producer Responsibility: Incentivizing Design Change
caseStudies.content:
France's eco-modulated Extended Producer Responsibility system, implemented since 2012, represents another documented game transformation that directly addresses the design phase of plastic products. This system adjusts the fees paid by producers based on the recyclability of their packaging. For instance, producers using carbon black plastics, which are invisible to optical sorting systems in material recovery facilities, face a financial malus or surcharge. Conversely, producers utilizing mono-material, lightweight, and easily recyclable designs receive a bonus or fee reduction. This mechanism creates a direct price signal that links product design decisions to their environmental outcomes, forcing designers to internalize end-of-life costs at the drawing-board stage rather than at the waste management stage.

The welfare impact of this system primarily reduces costs in the waste management channel and partially in the human health channel by incentivizing the removal of toxic additives. The estimated impact for France is 1 billion to 3 billion euros per year. This intervention operates at a national authority tier, with the European Union Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation framework providing a supranational mandate that will extend eco-modulation to all 27 member states by 2030. This creates a harmonized market signal reaching 30 percent of global gross domestic product. The French system is considered structural because it fundamentally alters product design incentives. Its success is evident in the measurable shift toward clear polyethylene terephthalate and mono-polyethylene formats across the French packaging industry. This case study demonstrates that targeted financial incentives can drive significant upstream changes in product design, moving beyond mere waste management to address the root causes of plastic pollution at the material and design level.

caseStudies.title: The East Palestine, Ohio Train Derailment: A Microcosm of Environmental Justice Costs
caseStudies.content:
The East Palestine, Ohio train derailment in February 2023 serves as a stark real-world example illustrating the acute and localized costs associated with the environmental justice channel of plastics production. This incident involved the release of 1.1 million pounds of vinyl chloride, a Group 1 carcinogen, into the environment. Vinyl chloride monomer is a key precursor in the production of polyvinyl chloride, a common plastic. The derailment necessitated the evacuation of thousands of residents and resulted in widespread contamination of air, soil, and water, with profound and lasting impacts on the health and livelihoods of the affected community.

The costs incurred in East Palestine, while not fully monetized in the paper's aggregate environmental justice channel, are representative of the damages that accrue in fenceline communities near petrochemical facilities globally. These include immediate health impacts from toxic exposure, long-term cancer risks, property value depression, and the psychological burden of living in a contaminated environment. The incident highlighted the vulnerability of communities to the hazards of petrochemical production and transportation, underscoring that the environmental justice channel is not confined to the Global South but is a pervasive issue wherever petrochemicals are produced, processed, or transported. The event demonstrated that the welfare costs associated with the inequitable distribution of harm from plastics production are immediate, severe, and often borne by communities with limited political and economic power. This case study reinforces the necessity of including environmental justice as a distinct and significant welfare-cost channel, even when precise global monetization remains challenging.

policyAnalysis.currentFramework:
The current regulatory and legal framework governing plastics is fragmented, inconsistent, and largely inadequate to address the systemic welfare costs quantified by the System Asset Pricing Model. Jurisdiction is distributed across multiple levels of government—municipal, national, and supranational—and involves various agencies and legal instruments, none of which comprehensively internalize the full 6.67 dollars of system welfare destruction per dollar of resin revenue. At the municipal level, policies often focus on downstream waste management, such as plastic bag bans, expanded curbside recycling programs, and local litter abatement initiatives. While these interventions can reduce visible pollution, they address less than 3 percent of the total system welfare cost, primarily impacting the waste management channel. For example, municipal bag levies in the United Kingdom have achieved 95–98 percent reductions in bag use, but their impact on the broader welfare gap is negligible. National frameworks vary widely. In the United States, the Environmental Protection Agency has jurisdiction over air and water pollution from petrochemical facilities under the Clean Air Act and Clean Water Act, and over hazardous waste under the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act. The Food and Drug Administration regulates plastics in food contact materials. However, these regulations typically address specific pollutants or stages of the lifecycle, failing to account for the cumulative, persistent, and transboundary nature of plastic pollution. The Federal Trade Commission's Green Guides provide guidance on environmental marketing claims, but enforcement against deceptive 'recyclable' labeling has been limited. The American Chemistry Council, a powerful industry lobby, has successfully opposed federal legislation for national plastic bag bans, mandatory recycled content, and extended producer responsibility. In the European Union, the framework is more advanced. The Single-Use Plastics Directive, adopted in 2019, bans certain single-use plastic items (e.g., straws, stirrers) and sets ambitious collection targets (90 percent for beverage bottles by 2029) and recycled content mandates (30 percent for plastic bottles by 2030). The upcoming Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation will further extend eco-modulated extended producer responsibility schemes across all member states. These supranational directives represent a more structural approach, addressing design and material flows, and are estimated to reduce welfare costs in the waste, climate, and health channels. Germany's deposit return scheme, achieving a 98 percent return rate for beverage containers, is a national success story within this broader European Union framework. Internationally, the United Nations Environment Assembly Resolution 5/14 (2022) mandated negotiations for a legally binding instrument on plastic pollution. This Intergovernmental Negotiating Committee process aims to establish a global treaty, potentially including binding production caps, design requirements, and financing mechanisms. However, as of mid-2026, these negotiations are stalled due to opposition from a bloc of petrostates, preventing the adoption of binding production reduction measures. The current framework, therefore, is characterized by a patchwork of local efforts, fragmented national regulations, and a stalled international process, none of which collectively address the systemic nature and scale of plastic's welfare destruction.
